title: When Doctors Don't Believe Your Pain
dek: You’ve been told your anger makes the pain worse. The body remembers otherwise. Here, fury is not a pathology—it’s a compass pointing to what medical disbelief has tried to bury. This is no guide to positive thinking; it’s a deep tissue reckoning with the rage that keeps you alive when no one listens.
bodyMarkdown:
Your anger makes your pain worse.

The doctor said it with a smile you could fold into a blade. Your file lay open on the desk, the same file that listed your symptoms in bullet points that never added up to a diagnosis. You felt the heat rise in your chest, a slow seep of fury that pooled just under your collarbone. You compressed it. Swallowed. And the pain, the one you’d come here to have taken seriously, sharpened beneath the silence. The body remembers this primal scene: the white coat, the laminated eye chart, the way your throat tightened around words that were never going to be heard. Not because you lacked clarity, but because the room had already decided what your body’s voice was worth.

## The Triage Room as an Interrogation

The questions weren’t really questions. “Are you sure it’s not stress?” “Have you considered that exercise might help?” “Is there a possibility this is emotional?” Each one a needle slipped under the skin of your credibility, extracting not blood but dignity. You left clutching a referral to a therapist you never wanted and a prescription for a muscle relaxant that dulled nothing except your will to push back. At home, you stared at the ceiling, your hip throbbing in its own language, and wondered if maybe they were right. Maybe the whole thing was manufactured. But the body doesn’t lie in words; it lies in electric signals, in the way your spine curves into a question mark when you try to explain. The fact that you’re still searching for answers after the third, the fifth, the seventh dismissal tells you something your chart never will: you are not the unreliable narrator here.

Most people who have been told their pain is a fiction develop a second awareness, a hyper-vigilance that scans their own sensations for fraudulence. Your jaw clenches in the waiting room. Your palms sweat through the paper gown. This is not anxiety disordered—it’s the body preparing for battle with a system that has already lost interest. Soraya Chemaly has pointed out that anger is an assertion of self-worth, a boundary signal that something unacceptable is happening. When a doctor labels that anger as “anxiety,” they are silencing the body’s most intelligent alarm. You learn to apologize for your own diagnostic instinct, to present your symptoms with a question mark at the end, hoping that deference will earn you care. It rarely does.

## The Body Remembers the Sentence It Was Given

You can feel it when you walk out of the clinic. That place between your shoulder blades where shame hardens into a knot, a small hot stone that spreads every time you replay the words. *Maybe it’s just my posture. Maybe I’m exaggerating.* The dismissal doesn’t stay in the realm of thought; it sinks into tissue. Gabor Maté has written that when the psyche cannot contain the “no,” the no migrates down into the body—manifesting as autoimmune chaos, chronic pelvic pain, migraine. The dismissal itself becomes a pathogen. Not metaphorically. Your body treats the disbelief as a threat, a version of the social death that our ancient nervous systems still recognize as fatal. Your cortisol spikes. Your fascia remembers the shape of being small.

If you try to name this, you might speak of a pressure behind your sternum, a damp weight in your pelvis, a tremor in your left thigh that never fully resolves. Pat Ogden describes how the body’s procedural memory—the movement patterns of survival—gets lodged in posture and breath. When your pain is invalidated repeatedly, your shoulders might roll inward, a thoracic cringe that says *I will protect what I can’t prove.* Your breath hitches high in the chest, a shallow rhythm that tells the central nervous system: danger is still here. The doctor may have walked out of the room, but your body hasn’t stopped trying to make you visible. The rage that you’ve been told is the problem is actually the body’s attempt to mobilize against erasure.

## Why Your Anger Is Not a Symptom

The equation is so tidy it almost sounds compassionate: solve the anger, solve the pain. It gives the physician an exit, a way to hand you back to yourself with a clean conscience. But anger isn’t the heat you need to ice. It’s the smoke that tells you where the fire burns. In medical settings, women, trans people, and people of color are especially likely to have their anger registered as hysteria or noncompliance. Your fury is pathologized before your synovial fluid is ever analyzed. Chemaly notes that anger is fundamentally a claim to personhood—the declaration that “my experience matters.” So when a provider tells you to calm down, they’re not soothing you; they’re asking you to surrender your only tool of protest.

You’ve probably noticed that the rage flares most intensely in the moments you feel most helpless. This isn’t a glitch. Helplessness is the cage, and anger is the body slamming itself against the bars. The sensation—heat rushing to your face, teeth clamping, vision narrowing—is a signal that you have not given up. If you had truly resigned, you’d feel nothing. The fury that frightens you is proof your nervous system still believes you deserve protection. The trick isn’t to extinguish it but to translate it before it seeps into the joints as grinding ache or into the gut as spasm. The body already knows how to convert anger into action; it’s just that the action was stolen from you in tenth grade, or in your first marriage, or the moment you learned that nice girls don’t yell.

## The Counterintuitive Analgesic

Here’s the claim nobody in a white coat will hand you on a discharge slip: anger, when given the right kind of space, can actually reduce physical pain. Not by the mechanism of distraction, but because the nervous system calms once it receives the message that the threat has been named. The body has been sounding a burglar alarm indefinitely; rage is the button that says *message received, stand down.* In a 2020 experimental pain study, Dr. Annmarie Cano found that patients with chronic pain who directly expressed their anger in therapy reported greater reductions in pain intensity than those who practiced only relaxation. Expression, not suppression, was the analgesic. Your grandmother knew this when she threw the good china.

If the edge of anger feels like a wire tightening in your stomach, you might find that a calmative like [Nature's Way Passion Flower (100 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00028LZ2G?tag=spankyspinola-20) takes the sharpness out without muting the message. This isn’t about numbing; it’s about dialing down the ambient alarm so you can hear what the fury needs to say. Passion flower works on GABA receptors, slowing the neurological cascade just enough that you can sit with the rage instead of being flooded by it. Combined with even a minute of deliberate, vibrating exhale—the kind that makes your lips flutter—you give the vagus nerve a signal that the threat has passed. This isn’t wellness-blog breathing. It’s biomechanical intervention.

## How Gaslighting Reshapes Your Spine

Stand in front of a mirror right now. Look at the way your head sits on your neck. Is it jutting slightly forward, as if you’re perpetually trying to hear something? Are your shoulders uneven, your pelvis tilted back to brace for a blow that never comes? Medical gaslighting doesn’t just live in memory; it sculptures the body. The constant bracing against disbelief creates a specific architecture: tight psoas muscles from the startle reflex that never unclenches, a throat that holds a permanent half-sob, a left-sided jaw click from years of biting back language. Ogden calls this the “procedural memory of the powerless,” and she documents how it carves physical pain patterns that no MRI can trace.

You can feel it now if you slow down. Place a hand on the back of your neck where the skull meets the spine. Is there a quality of density, a hardness like old grief? If you were to describe that sensation to the doctor who dismissed you, you might say *it feels like I’m carrying a weight I can’t name.* But here, you can name it: that density is the physical echo of every conversation where your truth was interrogated and found wanting. The tissues there have learned to armor the part of you that dared to speak. You might notice that you tilt your chin up slightly when talking about your symptoms, as if preparing for contradiction. The body’s adaptation to disbelief becomes its own prison, but a prison can be mapped. Start with where you brace, then trace the line.

## A Five-Minute Practice for When They Won’t Listen

You don’t need a yoga mat or a silent room. You need your own two feet and a wall if you’re unsteady. This practice is about reclaiming the vertical axis, the right to take up space in your full height.

Stand with your back against a wall, feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Press the whole length of your spine into the wall—sacrum, thoracic curve, back of the skull. Notice the parts that resist. Now shift your weight forward so only your sacrum stays in contact, and let your arms hang heavy. Breathe in through your nose and on the exhale, push a silent “no” out of your mouth, jaw slack, no sound. Let the exhale be longer than the inhale, a slow release of air that doesn’t try to be pretty. Do this five times.

Now step away from the wall. Plant your feet and feel the floor pushing back. Say aloud, in a tone that doesn’t need to be loud, “I believe myself.” If your voice cracks, let it crack. If tears come, don’t wipe them. The body registers spoken assertion as a corrective signal to the brainstem—a somatic declaration of agency. Ogden would call this a “resource action,” a small, physical reclaiming of the boundary that was violated. Afterward, you might drink a glass of cold water very slowly, feeling it travel down, because hydration after a threat state reminds the gut that the fight is over.

## FAQ: The Questions You Don’t Need to Apologize For

**What if my doctor says I just need to relax?**
Your doctor is confusing a threat response with a lifestyle choice. Relaxation isn’t something you can command when your nervous system has been taught that speaking up earns punishment. You can, however, signal safety through direct body-up techniques: an adaptogen like [Gaia Herbs Holy Basil Leaf (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0013OXDFS?tag=spankyspinola-20) may help keep cortisol from spiking every time you need to make a follow-up call. Holy basil supports adrenal equilibrium so you don’t crash after the fight. But the deeper repair comes when you stop trying to convince someone who has already categorized you. You conserve your voice for the ones who listen.

**How do I stop the rage from worsening my symptoms?**
The rage doesn’t worsen your symptoms; the stifling of it does. Start by giving the anger a shape and a location each time it rises. Say to yourself, *I am feeling a hot, clenching ball in my right upper chest.* That alone moves the experience from limbic chaos to prefrontal naming. Then, let the body complete the defensive action it was prevented from taking. If you wanted to push the doctor away, push against a wall now. If you wanted to run, stomp your feet ten times. The anger drains not through calm, but through completion. Reading [The Dance of Anger — Harriet Lerner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0062957031?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help you see that your fury is not a monolith but a relational pattern that can be rewritten without losing its truth.

**Can I ask for a different provider without being labeled difficult?**
Yes, and the label is not your problem. Your file may say “challenging patient,” but what you’re doing is protecting your own body’s integrity. You can simply state: “I’m looking for a collaborative approach, and I’ll be seeking a second opinion.” Don’t explain. Don’t justify. You are not on trial. The doctor’s frustration with your autonomy is an admission that the system depends on your passivity. Your anger is the boundary you didn’t know you could draw with jet-black ink.

## The Rage That Connects You to Every Other Unbelievved Body

Sit with this: your anger is not your private pathology. It belongs to a vast, subterranean network of people sitting in exam rooms right now with the same tight throat, the same clenched fists hidden under the paper gown. Your fury is a transmission from the collective body of the dismissed—the women whose endometriosis was called “bad periods,” the Black patients whose pain was rated lower because the resident’s chart had no box for systemic bias, the queer folks whose autoimmune flares were chalked up to “lifestyle.” This anger is not a tantrum. It is historical data, a lineage of mouths told to soften themselves while their spines went rigid with the effort of containing a scream.

When you let yourself feel it, really feel it—not from a safe remove, but in the way that makes your pelvis shake and your eyes film over—you’re not breaking down. You’re breaking through the insulation that keeps you isolated from the truth that your body is coherent, your suffering is legible, and your rage is the thread that sews you back into the resistance. This isn’t about becoming a better patient. It’s about becoming a witness to your own bodily testimony, fierce enough that the next time a doctor’s eyes skate past your pain, you will know it’s not your story that’s missing—it’s their capacity to hear it. For more on reclaiming the body’s truth after dismissal, the companion journal at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) holds raw reflections that don’t ask you to translate your fury into something polite. You’ll find no tidy resolutions there, only the echo of someone else’s spine straightening, a sound your own body already knows.
